segments.title: How castle engineering multiplies defensive power
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke presents a castle as an engineered force multiplier that lets a small garrison defend against a much larger attacking force.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains that castles harness available resources, engineering, and architecture to create a defensible structure. Their central advantage is force multiplication: a small number of defenders can resist a much larger attacking force. He presents that mismatch between garrison and attacker as the source of his fascination with castles. The passage is a compact introduction to how fortification design turns construction into military advantage.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke describes castles as large engineering and architectural works that allow a minimum number of defenders to resist a maximum number of attackers.

segments.title: Why medieval sea battles resembled land battles
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke compares medieval naval engagements with land battles, arguing that the fighting was often transferred from land to combat between ships.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke moves from castles to land battles and emphasizes their similarities with combat at sea. For the medieval period, he describes naval engagements as land battles fought from ship to ship. He is making a comparison about how fighting was organized, rather than claiming that land and naval environments were identical. The short explanation gives a useful interpretive frame for why medieval sea fighting could look familiar to a student of land warfare.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke says he enjoys the similarities between land and sea battles and describes medieval sea battles as land battles fought from ship to ship.
